# ARC-QUIC-RFC8999-0001 - QUIC RFC 8999 Invariants Architecture

## Purpose

Describe how the shared QUIC packet parsing and packet modeling surface
preserves the version-independent invariants from RFC 8999 while staying
small enough to avoid baking in version-specific behavior.

## Scope

This design covers the long-header substrate handled by
[`QuicPacketParser`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicPacketParser.cs), the
parsed long-header and version-negotiation packet models, the preservation of
opaque control bits and trailing payload data, and the connection-ID length and
size rules that are common across the RFC 8999 slice.

It does not define later RFC 9000 short-header semantics or packet-level
version-specific extensions outside the shared long-header invariant surface.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0001
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0002
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0003
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0004
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0005
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0006
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0007
- REQ-QUIC-RFC8999-S5P1-0008

## Design Summary

The shared packet parser classifies the packet form from the first-byte high
bit and delegates to packet-specific helpers that preserve the remainder of the
wire shape.

## Key Components

- [`QuicPacketParser`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicPacketParser.cs)
- [`QuicPacketParsing`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicPacketParsing.cs)
- [`QuicLongHeaderPacket`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicLongHeaderPacket.cs)
- [`QuicVersionNegotiationPacket`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicVersionNegotiationPacket.cs)
- [`QuicHeaderForm`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicHeaderForm.cs)
- [`QuicVariableLengthInteger`](../../../src/Incursa.Quic/QuicVariableLengthInteger.cs)

## Data and State Considerations

- The form bit controls packet classification.
- The remaining seven control bits stay opaque to the shared layer.
- Connection-ID length bytes are preserved on the wire and bounded by 255
  bytes.
- The version-specific remainder is preserved as trailing opaque data.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- Truncated long headers should fail early.
- Shared wire-shape tests may be dual-tagged when they prove both RFC 8999 and later RFC 9000 clauses.
- The architecture should stay narrow enough that version-specific semantics can evolve without rewriting the shared parser model.

## Alternatives Considered

- Fold all packet variants into one parser type and infer version-specific semantics in the shared layer.

## Risks

- Over-broad shared parsing would make it harder to keep RFC 8999 invariants separate from later RFC 9000 packet semantics.
- Overlapping tests need deliberate requirement tags so shared coverage is not miscounted.

## Open Questions

- Which future packet slices should reuse this shared wire-shape model without inheriting the long-header-only invariants?
